title: Annual Attrition
content:
Annual attrition is calculated as:
Attrition rate = (Number of employee separations / average number of employees) x 100.

title: Annual Salary
content: The annual cost associated with an employee&rsquo;s pay and benefits. For calculating total impact of attrition, we recommend leveraging a loaded salary figure which includes the cost of benefits. If your loaded annual salary incorporates costs associated with IT, HR and other support organizations, ensure you do not double count those in Onboarding Costs .

title: Changes to the Future of Workforce Management
content:
The Drivers Behind the Need for Change This standard focuses on intersecting drivers behind the need for developing an entirely new approach to the Workforce Management function. Those areas include:
The exponential advancements in technology. The global pandemic, COVID-19. The dramatic growth in independent workers &amp; the gig economy. The introduction of a new generation of workers, “Generation Z.” More extraordinary times of business uncertainty. Exponential Advancements in Technology For …

title: Demand calculation
content:
A simple demand calculation leveraged across the industry to generate an approximate base FTE required is as follows:
Base FTE Required = Call Volume Offered X Average Handle Time / 3600 / Total Annual Work Hours / Occupancy / (1 - Shrinkage)
This calculation can be leveraged to establish a base FTE needed to service an annual workload in order to begin calculating the true costs associated with employee attrition, as described in the High Cost of Attrition

title: Discrete-Event vs. Monte Carlo Simulation Models
content:
Discrete-Event vs. Monte Carlo simulation models represent two fundamental approaches to modeling contact center operations, capacity planning, and performance optimization. While both methods use probabilistic modeling to handle uncertainty, they differ significantly in their temporal perspective, computational approach, and practical applications within workforce management systems.
Overview Definition and Core Concepts Discrete-Event Simulation models contact center operations as a sequence …

content:

title: Event Management
content:
Event Management is a systematic methodology for detecting, classifying, responding to, and resolving operational incidents that impact contact center performance and customer service delivery. Event management serves as the foundational process for Resource Optimization Center (ROC) operations, providing structured protocols for maintaining service level objectives and ensuring consistent operational excellence.
The event management framework enables organizations to transition from reactive …

title: Future WFM Operating Standard
content:
Introduction This document aims to introduce a new standard for workforce management (WFM) and the approach used to successfully support the future of contact center operations. While all contact centers and WFM teams have variations in how they conduct forecasting, scheduling, and real-time management, this standard seeks to recommend a new approach to a profession that is due for an overhaul.
We present why now is the time for change and then outline a future for workforce management …

title: Interpersonal Relationships
content:
The future WFM standard proposes a new employee-first framework, where interpersonal relationships between WFM, Finance, HR, Contact Center management, executive leadership and frontline employees are significantly enhanced. The framework views WFM in a new light, where employees are prioritized first and how automation and simulation technologies can be leveraged to benefit employees.
WFM’s direct involvement with employee satisfaction and retention will introduce a radically different …

title: Length of Training
content: Length of training is the total time from beginning training class to an employee going into production. It is up to individual organizations and the calculation being leverages as to whether to include nesting periods as part of training length, or as part of production.

title: Level 1 Process Templates
content:
Sample SOPs for Level 1 This packet turns the rhythm from Section 7.8 into three one-page SOPs you can run on Monday. They are instruments, not paperwork: light, repeatable, and written in the language of the floor. Map them directly to the cadence — week → day → interval — so the same work happens the same way twice.

title: Multi-Objective Optimization in Contact Center
content:
Multi-objective optimization in contact centers is the mathematical process of simultaneously optimizing multiple, often conflicting objectives to achieve the best possible overall business outcome. Unlike traditional approaches that focus on a single metric (typically service level or cost), this approach balances service quality, operational efficiency, agent wellness, and revenue generation.
Overview The Four Pillars of Contact Center Optimization Modern contact centers must balance four …

title: Next Generation Routing
content: Next-Generation Routing Next-Generation Routing represents a paradigm shift from traditional skills-based routing to intelligent workload orchestration powered by real-time operational context. Unlike conventional routing systems that rely on static rules and historical data, next-generation routing leverages Model Context Protocol (MCP) to access live system state across the entire contact center ecosystem, enabling autonomous decision-making that optimizes for agent wellness, business …

title: Onboarding Costs
content:
Onboarding costs are an important factor to consider when calculating the full financial impact that attrition has on an organization. While some expenses may be hard dollars (example, paying an external recruiting firm), other expenses may be though of as soft dollars, such as employees time spent supporting IT and HR requirements.
In considering the full cost of attrition to an organization, WFM Labs recommends establishing an average cost per agent to capture both hard and soft dollar …

title: Power of One
content: Achieving Service Level WFM teams strive to meet service level goals throughout every interval of the day, even when unexpected situations arise. Automation is critical for intraday management tactics to mitigate unforeseen events such as frontline staff calling in sick, weather events, sudden technical troubles, or unexpectedly high call volumes. While WFM teams take action to make adjustments to maintaining service levels in the face of these events, WFM teams can not respond quickly enough …

title: Real-Time Cause and Effect Fishbone
content:
Cause and Effect Fishbone Diagram Ishikawa diagrams (also called fishbone diagrams or cause-and-effect diagrams) are diagrams that show the causes of a certain event. Common uses of the Ishikawa diagram are product design and quality defect prevention, to identify potential factors causing an overall effect.1
The ROC&rsquo;s use of the Real-Time Cause and Effect Diagram allows consistent diagnosis of what factors are causing service level objectives to not be met, when thresholds have been …

title: Resource Optimization Center (ROC)
content:
Resource Optimization Center (ROC) A Resource Optimization Center (ROC) is a centralized command and control environment designed to provide real-time operational intelligence and systematic variance management for contact center operations. The ROC serves as the foundational framework for achieving operational excellence through proactive monitoring, incident management, and dynamic resource optimization across the entire workforce management ecosystem.
The ROC concept emerged from the …

title: Speed to proficiency curve
content:
The speed to proficiency curve expresses a relationship between the Average Handle Time for an employee when they begin taking calls against the months involved before they become &ldquo;proficient&rdquo; - or able to handle calls at the same efficiency as an employee who is said to be tenured.
In the simplest method, we can plot that in a linear relationship on two axis, where X represents the months required to become proficient, and Y represents the average handle time:

title: Technology
content: Technology supporting contact center organizations has evolved since the earliest call distributors of the 1960&rsquo;s. For purposes of the Future WFM Operating Standard, we outline a slice of the overall ecosystem, touching on technologies that have a strong, interconnected relationship with the goals, roles, processes and interpersonal relationship approach of standard. This section acknowledges there are many parallel technology systems the WFM team may leverage or interface with. An …

title: Training Attrition
content:
Training attrition references the % of people who leave at some point after starting training, but prior to entering full production. The calculation for training attrition is:
Training Attrition % = (Number of new hires who exit / Total New Hires) X 100
Training attrition can be calculated at an individual class level, by instructor, by business segment or across an entire organization.

title: WFM Assessment
content:
Introduction The WFMLabs.org maturity curve offers a comprehensive framework for assessing call centers&rsquo; operational efficiency and technological advancement. This article examines the distribution of call centers across various maturity levels, emphasizing the prevalence of lower levels (1 and 2) and the scarcity of higher levels (3, 4, and 5) in the current market.

content:

title: WFM Labs Erlang-O™
content: WFM Labs Erlang-O™ WFM Labs Erlang-O™ is a modern staffing framework developed by Ted Lango at WFM Labs to address the limitations of traditional staffing models like Erlang-C and Erlang-A, which often break down under the operational realities of today’s contact centers. WFM Labs Erlang-O™ is not a replacement for a base staffing algorithm; rather, it is an overlay framework that enhances any interval-level staffing calculation by embedding adjustments for variance, volatility, and intraday …

title: WFM Labs Risk Score™
content:
WFM Labs Risk Score™ The WFM Labs Risk Score™ is a proprietary framework designed to quantify the operational risk embedded within your capacity plans, providing leaders with a clear, intuitive indicator of the balance between service level protection, employee satisfaction, and cost control. It enables WFM teams to communicate capacity risk in business terms, empowering the C-Suite to make more confident staffing decisions in the face of demand uncertainty.

title: WFM Roles
content:
WFM Organizational Roles — 5-Level Maturity Map This WFM standard outlines the organizational roles that carry the loop of forecast → schedule → real-time → learn. Roles are grouped into Forecasting, Scheduling, and Real-Time, with cross-cutting partners (platform/automation, routing, vendor management, analytics). What changes through the five levels is not the existence of these roles, but **who owns what, the artifacts they produce, and how decisions are made and executed**.
How to Use This …

title: Wiki:About
content: WFM Labs is an organization formed by workforce management &amp; customer service professionals seeking to reinvent the next-generation approach to successfully operating contact centers. The community examines the workforce management framework in a new light, where employees are prioritized first and where automation &amp; simulation technologies can be leveraged to benefit employees while delivering superior results to customers &amp; shareholders.

title: Workforce Management Standard Introduction
content: What is Workforce Management? To anyone not closely aligned with the functions of traditional contact center workforce management, let’s outline the principles behind the job function. In the simplest sense, all customer service organizations attempt to align supply and demand, where “demand” is represented by customers seeking service, and “supply” is represented by the employees providing service. Whether in a physical or virtual queue seeking service, a universally understood dynamic plays …
